review_text: Not such a great way to fly. I booked an air ticket and the flight departure timing was brought forward by 7 hours. We had the option to accept, reschedule or cancel to get a refund. I chose the third option but was put thru multiple individuals who do not seem to know what they are doing (each contradicting the other) but eventually, I received an email to say the refund was being processed and it would take 6 weeks. Well, it was the 6th week yesterday and I wrote in to enquire, only to be told they will be processing it now. Today I received another email (a proper refund notice this time) to say it will be another 6 weeks for the refund to come thru.
issue: Flight departure time was moved up by 7 hours, causing inconvenience.
fixes: Deploy a real‑time passenger notification API (e.g., Twilio Notify) linked to the airline’s reservation system so any schedule change triggers an automated SMS/WhatsApp message with the new departure time, expected delay, and alternative flight options within 10 minutes of the change. | - Create a “Schedule Flexibility” rule in the crew‑management software (such as Amadeus Altéa Crew) that automatically re‑assigns standby pilots or shifts from adjacent flights when a departure slot is rescheduled by more than 5 hours, ensuring at least one backup crew member per affected aircraft. | - Build a compensation calculator into the post‑flight survey workflow (using SurveyMonkey) that awards eligible passengers (those whose travel plans were disrupted >2 hours) with a pre‑approved $50 e‑voucher instantly, eliminating manual reimbursement claims. | - Integrate a predictive analytics model (e.g., Azure Machine Learning) with historical weather and traffic data to forecast potential schedule adjustments ≥6 hours ahead; if the model predicts a high likelihood, trigger a preemptive email alert to impacted customers via Salesforce Marketing Cloud before the official change notice is published.

fixes: Deploy a dedicated refund workflow in Zendesk Suite (or ServiceNow) with automated SLA alerts that trigger after 48 hours without manual intervention; configure the system to escalate unresolved refunds to a senior analyst within 24 hours. | - Implement a real‑time audit dashboard in Power BI that pulls daily status updates from the ERP (e.g., SAP) and flags any ticket older than five business days, prompting immediate follow‑up via email/SMS notifications to the responsible team lead. | - Create a “Rapid Refund” queue in Salesforce CPQ with a pre‑approved $500 limit and a 72‑hour service level agreement, staffed by a cross‑functional rapid‑refund squad who complete these cases via a standardized form in Google Forms. | - Establish a weekly KPI report on average refund turnaround time (RTT) in Tableau, set a target RTT ≤ 7 days, and automatically route any case exceeding the threshold to a root‑cause analysis Slack channel where corrective actions are logged and tracked.

review_text: The check-in process at JFK was the worst check-in experience I've experienced. I was waiting for check-in for over 2 hours. The line just didn't move at all. The agent was extremely inefficient. I was one of the last customers to check in and it was about 30 minutes before the departure time. The counter agents was escorting a group of last-to-check-in customers to the security checkpoint so they could skip the line (we were late) and somehow I was the only one who left in the security line on my own. The service on board was terrible despite the high-regarded in-flight service that Singapore Airlines is usually known for. The service was so slow for a flight with only 160 customers.  I sat at one of the single seat toward the end of the aircraft and the steward kept bumping into me like I was sitting on the aisle. He didn't even apologize. My food was OK at best. The worst part was probably the lavatories. Truly disgusting, trash was everywhere on the floor.
issue: Check-in wait time exceeded two hours and the line moved very slowly, with inefficient agents.
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based queue management platform (e.g., QLess) integrated with the property’s PMS to push guests into virtual lines and display real‑time estimated wait times on digital screens, reducing physical congestion by at least 30 % within four weeks. | - Install self‑service kiosks equipped with QR code scanning and pre‑arrival check‑in modules (e.g., Amadeus Kiosk Suite) so arriving guests can complete registration before reaching the front desk, cutting average line length by up to 40 %. | - Implement a workforce‑management solution such as Kronos Workforce Central to forecast peak arrival windows and schedule additional “quick‑check” agents who receive 15‑minute micro‑learning videos via Loom each week; track a 20 % decrease in average agent handle time after three months. | - Introduce a mobile guest service app (using existing technology like GuestU) that allows guests to submit ID and room preferences from their cars, automatically creating a reservation slot and triggering a staff notification via Slack, thereby eliminating manual data entry during high‑traffic periods.

review_text: Siem Reap to Kolkata via Singapore. We chose Singapore as we had heard lots of wonderful things about the airline. Our experience was worse than any bucket-shop airlines and we thought Singapore was a world standard airline. On the Siem Reap to Singapore Route my seat had a broken table which I was informed only on the way to the boarding the flight when I was suddenly being pressurised by their ground staff. Although I was waiting at the airport near the gate for over an hour none of your ground staff bothered to discuss this matter with me.  Furthermore the table was only secured with sticky tape which kept falling on my lap and on a couple of occasions fell on my watch! We then received further shoddy service from ground staff at Singapore on the Kolkata flight. Having paid USD 700+ for our one-way ticket from REP to CCU I was shocked to be downgraded to an airline with filthy seats, no TV, no leg space and in general badly in need of an uplift. To cap it all the cabin staff offered me gifts worth SGD 75 on the plane. This offer got progressively downgraded to a few miles on their airline.
issue: Seat had a broken table that was only disclosed at boarding and was poorly secured with sticky tape, causing it to fall on the passenger’s lap and watch
fixes: Deploy a mandatory pre‑boarding inspection checklist in iAuditor (SafetyCulture) that includes a “Table Integrity” step; any defect is logged and triggers an immediate crew alert to replace the seat or secure it with a certified fastener before boarding. | - Install QR‑code stickers on every seat back that link to a short video demonstrating proper table attachment; passengers scan the code during boarding to verify correct installation, and non‑compliant seats are flagged in the cabin management system for immediate repair. | - Create a real‑time “Seat Condition Dashboard” in Power BI that pulls data from the maintenance work order system (e.g., SAP MM) and displays the number of seats with defective tables per flight, allowing operations managers to take corrective actions within 24 hours. | - Implement a post‑flight “Seat Damage Incident Report” form hosted on ServiceNow that requires crew to record any loose parts, attach photos, and assign a ticket to the maintenance team; the report automatically generates a KPI dashboard and initiates a root‑cause analysis workflow.

review_text: We were given a flight with Scoot from Bangkok to Singapore, and then with Singapore to Auckland. The flight with Scoot was perfectly acceptable but what was not was that they decided that we must collect our boarding pass in Singapore which might have been OK but they they never told us where to collect them from, so we we spent ages trying to find the location and only just made it to the gate in time. The previous leg from Milan to Hanoi involved a Scoot flight and we received all our passes in Milan. On the flight when meals were served the crew just appeared at the seat with a tray whilst we were dozing and expected us to put out the table. As we had ordered book the cook, the main meal service took a while and we had finished our meal before drinks were offered. Premium economy food was no better than Economy fare and considering the cost of the flight a little more thought could have gone into the meal.
issue: Passengers were not informed where to collect their boarding passes in Singapore, causing confusion and a rushed arrival at the gate.
fixes: Deploy digital wayfinding kiosks (e.g., SITA Wayfinder) at check‑in counters that display real‑time “Boarding Pass Pickup” instructions for each departing flight, automatically updating when the passenger’s status changes. | - Integrate the airport’s mobile app with the airline’s reservation API so that passengers receive a push notification 15 minutes before departure containing a QR code to scan at the designated boarding pass collection point. | - Add a mandatory “boarding pass pickup location” field to all online booking confirmations and email reminders, and configure Salesforce Service Cloud to auto‑populate the corresponding zone ID on the passenger’s itinerary page. | - Conduct a weekly 10‑minute “Gate‑Ready” huddle using Microsoft Teams where the ground operations team reviews any upcoming flights flagged by the SITA Gate Management System for potential boarding pass confusion, and logs corrective actions in the Power BI dashboard.

review_text: The ground crew were unreasonable and very rude. We were denied boarding as according to them we were 4 minutes late.  The ground crew did a lousy job of offloading our luggage. We had to wait almost half a day in the baggage carousel before filling a missing bag report.
issue: Ground crew were rude and denied boarding for a minor 4‑minute delay
fixes: Deploy a real‑time delay‑notification app (e.g., ServiceNow FieldOps) linked to flight schedules so ground crew receive automatic alerts on tablets when a gate change exceeds 3 minutes; crew must acknowledge receipt before they can deny boarding. | - Require staff to complete a mandatory “De‑escalation Script” quiz in the LMS (Cornerstone OnDemand) with a minimum 90% pass rate every quarter, tracked via compliance dashboards. | - Introduce a post‑interaction survey via Qualtrics triggered within 24 hours of denied boarding, routing any score below 7/10 to a dedicated escalation queue for immediate manager review. | - Implement a weekly 15‑minute role‑play drill using video clips from recent incidents recorded on GoPro Hero 11 cameras, scored by supervisors through a shared Google Sheet rubric.

issue: Luggage was mishandled, leading to a half‑day wait and a missing bag report
fixes: Deploy RFID tags on all baggage and integrate them with the existing SITA Baggage Management System (BMS) to automatically alert ground staff when a tag is off‑route and trigger real‑time rerouting within 5 minutes of detection. | - Install self‑service kiosks at every carousel equipped with barcode scanners linked to the airline’s baggage tracking database; passengers can scan their claim codes, receive immediate status updates, and file a missing‑bag report instantly, cutting response time from hours to minutes. | - Implement a “Baggage Quality Assurance” workflow in the airline’s ServiceNow platform where every bag handled is logged, photographed, and digitally signed by the handler before it leaves the belt, creating a tamper‑evident audit trail that reduces mis‑routing errors by at least 40 %. | - Launch a predictive analytics dashboard in Power BI that forecasts peak luggage volumes per flight and schedules additional handlers in advance, ensuring a minimum staffing ratio of 1 handler per 20 bags during high‑traffic windows and reducing over‑capacity delays. | - Introduce a mobile‑enabled compensation workflow using Salesforce CPQ that issues instant e‑credits or vouchers upon a verified missing‑bag incident, enabling customers to complete the reimbursement process within 24 hours without needing to call the contact center.

review_text: Our first flight was delayed more than one hour, so we lost our connecting flight. The new booking provided was horrible. I was travelling with my four-year-old and the new seat provided is far away, and we were separated. All the family members are separated for the whole journey. Miscommunication between the crew members made us to walk between the terminals for 2 times. Didn't get any help from the crew, even though we requested to provide a seat together.
issue: Flight was delayed over an hour, causing a missed connecting flight
fixes: Deploy an automated delay‑management workflow in Amadeus Altéa (or SITA FlightOps) that triggers an instant rebooking offer via email/ SMS within 5 minutes of any delay ≥15 min, with the passenger’s preferred alternate flights pre‑loaded into the system. | - Install real‑time flight‑status push notifications on the airline’s mobile app (using Twilio SendGrid) so passengers receive updates as soon as gate changes occur, reducing reliance on manual announcements. | - Implement a “delay buffer” SOP where every departing flight receives a minimum 10‑minute extra holding time at the boarding gate, logged in the crew scheduling software (e.g., Sabre AirCentre), ensuring more connections are met even when scheduled times slip slightly. | - Create a Power BI dashboard linked to the airline’s operations center that flags any connection risk >5 min, automatically prompting dispatchers to reallocate ground staff and re‑sequence aircraft to minimize missed‑connection incidents.

issue: Rebooking after the missed connection was poorly handled
fixes: Deploy a self‑service rebooking portal integrated with Amadeus Altéa (or Sabre Red Workspace) that automatically generates new itineraries within 15 minutes of a missed connection, sending confirmation emails with QR codes for quick verification. | - Implement a “Missed Connection Escalation Rule” in Zendesk’s ticketing workflow that triggers a 5‑minute SLA for agent handoff when a passenger reports a delayed flight, routing the case to a dedicated “Connection Recovery” queue staffed by two senior agents per shift. | - Introduce a post‑flight SMS survey via Twilio that asks passengers about their rebooking experience; any score below 7/10 automatically creates a follow‑up task in Salesforce Service Cloud with a 24‑hour resolution target. | - Schedule weekly 20‑minute root‑cause analysis huddles using Miro boards where the operations team reviews the top three rebooking failures from the past week, logs corrective actions, and tracks improvement metrics on a shared Power BI dashboard.

fixes: Deploy an automated “Family Seat Guard” rule in the reservation system (e.g., Sabre) that prohibits assigning any two adult‑child pairs more than one row apart unless the booking is flagged with a “Child Traveling Alone” indicator. | - Integrate a seat‑map widget into the mobile app that lets passengers select adjacent seats at check‑in; if no adjacent seats are available, trigger an instant alert to the crew via the airline’s CrewConnect portal. | - Add a “Seat Together” filter in the seat‑selection screen of the website and mobile app that auto‑populates the top‑three rows of adjacent seats based on passenger age, allowing parents to instantly confirm the closest pairing. | - Configure the crew management software (e.g., Amadeus Altéa Crew) to display a real‑time “Family Separation Alert” whenever a boarding pass includes a child and a non‑family member assigned more than one row apart, prompting crew to re‑assign seats before boarding.

fixes: Deploy a real‑time crew communication board in the terminal using the Microsoft Teams “Planner” app, assigning each passenger group a unique task with start/end time and location; automatically flag any overlap for immediate reassignment. | - Integrate the airport’s operations management software (e.g., Amadeus Airport Operations) with a rule‑based routing engine that prevents crews from being assigned to two flights departing from the same gate within 15 minutes, and logs all violations for post‑flight review. | - Launch a mandatory 10‑minute pre‑departure huddle conducted via Zoom, recorded and stored in the airline’s LMS (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors) with a required attendance checklist that must be signed off before crew are cleared for duty. | - Install RFID‑enabled wristbands on passengers that sync with the crew’s handheld devices (such as Zebra TC56); when a passenger is tagged at a gate, the device alerts the crew that they have already boarded another flight, preventing double‑walks.

review_text: I embarked on a journey with high hopes and anticipation, having secured a connecting flight ticket from Penang to Singapore, and then onwards to Jakarta. Opting for Singapore Airlines, renowned for its outstanding reputation in both air travel and service, I anticipated nothing less than a seamless experience. The cost of this connecting flight amounted to MYR 1601, roughly USD 335, a price I was more than willing to pay for quality service. My journey commenced with a minor delay in takeoff from Penang. The flight proceeded smoothly, accompanied by a light snack comprising a slice of pizza and a beverage of choice. As we approached Changi Airport, the situation took an unexpected turn. The captain announced a delay in landing, estimating an additional 15 to 20 minutes before touchdown. A sense of unease crept over me as my connecting flight departure time loomed closer. Despite my concerns, I remained optimistic, trusting in the efficiency of Singapore Airlines to navigate any challenges seamlessly. Upon landing at 12:05 pm and disembarking at 12:18 pm, I was met with a startling realization. Rather than a smooth transition to my connecting flight, I was handed a new ticket, rescheduled for departure at 4:10 pm, accompanied by a SGD 15 food voucher for Burger King and Coffee Bean.  I sought clarity from the attendant, only to be met with dismissiveness. The absence of any acknowledgment or attempt at redress left me deeply disappointed. The disregard for my time and the indifferent handling of the situation underscored a gap in the airline's commitment to customer service. In retrospect, my expectation for exemplary hospitality and proactive problem-solving from a reputed airline like Singapore Airlines was regrettably unmet. As I reflect on this experience, I am reminded of the importance of genuine care and consideration in customer service, particularly in moments of inconvenience or distress.
issue: The landing delay caused a missed connecting flight and forced a rebooking to a much later departure, disrupting the travel itinerary.
fixes: Deploy an automated “Missed‑Connection” alert in the airline’s operations center (e.g., SITA Airline Management) that triggers within 10 minutes of a known landing delay, automatically offering eligible passengers a rebooking on the next available flight with real‑time seat inventory and issuing e‑tickets via Sabre Red Workspace. | - Implement a dynamic contingency routing engine in the airline’s reservation system (Amadeus Altéa) that generates alternate itineraries for affected passengers and sends push notifications through the mobile app (using Twilio SMS/WhatsApp API) before they reach the gate, ensuring immediate rebooking options are presented. | - Introduce a “Delay Compensation” workflow in Salesforce Service Cloud that auto‑calculates mileage credits or vouchers based on the delay duration and issues them instantly via email, reducing manual processing time from hours to minutes. | - Schedule weekly 15‑minute “Disruption Drill” calls between operations and passenger‑service teams, where the latest delay data is pulled from FlightAware APIs and discussed using a shared Google Sheet template to document corrective actions and track compliance metrics.

issue: The compensation offered was limited to a small food voucher, which was insufficient for the inconvenience caused by the missed connection.
fixes: Automated Compensation Engine:** Deploy a rule‑based decision tree in Salesforce Service Cloud that automatically calculates a “missed‑connection” compensation tier (e.g., $50 + a full refund) based on flight duration, fare class, and passenger age, then issues the voucher within 24 hours via email/SMS. | - **Real‑Time Customer Journey Dashboard:** Configure Power BI to pull live itinerary data from Amadeus APIs; when a connection is missed, trigger an alert that pushes a predefined compensation offer to the agent’s desktop chat widget, ensuring immediate resolution. | - **Proactive Rebooking + Upsell Workflow:** Use Zapier to create a workflow that, upon detecting a missed connection, auto‑books the next available flight and offers a complimentary upgrade bundle (economy to business) before the passenger reaches baggage claim. | - **Customer Satisfaction Survey & Escalation Routing:** Send a Qualtrics survey immediately after compensation delivery with a mandatory NPS question; route any score ≤ 6 to a dedicated “Inconvenience Recovery” queue in Zendesk where a senior specialist contacts the passenger within two hours.

review_text: The flight was booked at a time when the Covid situation was still looking better and the chance to be be able to leave Changi airport during a long layover looked good. That face with Singapore Airlines flexibility claim of change of flights on their website, made me booking a flight with a 16h layover. Unfortunately, things were not looking good about a month ago, and I tried to change my flights to a short layover. I tried to contact SQ via its change of flight form for Covid related reasons through their website. 5 requests over the span of a month remain unanswered till today, complaint emails were also not replied to. I also tried to contact SQ through their callcenter, was placed in a loop for hours. After getting through after days of trying, I was told that there were seats available for the same booking class and I would be informed once the the ticket would be re-issued. So far so good. An hour later I was called, saying that this „special fare ticket“ can only be changed if if I pay this and that in addition, which ended up to a ridiculous amount, and I declined. So I was back to square and had to endure a 16h stay at Changi airport. SQ does not honor their claim of ticket flexibility. Other carrier I flew during the pandemic were willing honor their flexibility claims. The flights themselves were rather unremarkable, not as polished as Qatar, with no dining on demand offered. Food was average to say the least. No touchscreen TV and average seat on the really noisy 777ER on the long flight. Overall due to the non existing customer service on the ground, not recommended
issue: Customer service was unresponsive; multiple contact attempts via website, email, and call center received no reply or long hold times.
fixes: Deploy a real‑time ticketing portal (e.g., Zendesk Suite) integrated with your CRM and auto‑assign tickets within 15 minutes of receipt, ensuring every inquiry triggers a documented response. | - Configure an automated escalation workflow in Zendesk that routes any ticket older than 24 hours to a senior agent group and sends a Slack notification to the team lead. | - Implement a “first‑contact resolution” KPI measured by a dashboard in Power BI that tracks average handle time per channel; set a target of ≤ 2 min for web/email inquiries and ≤ 30 seconds for phone calls. | - Launch a self‑service knowledge base on Confluence, linking it to the chatbot on your website so customers receive instant answers before escalating to live agents. | - Schedule weekly 20‑minute shift handoffs using Microsoft Teams Planner, where the outgoing shift logs open tickets and the incoming shift reviews them, cutting down on hold times and missed follow‑ups.

review_text: Check-in was fast, because I could use the business-class check-in counters as a PPS-member. Otherwise endless queues. Boarding was slow because the flight was full and I boarded late with the crowds. No recognition as a Solitaire-PPS-member on board as before. Cabin crew busy with 'sorting' the passengers and their cabin-luggage to not miss our slot. Seat in a 3-3-3 configuration very narrow, seat-pitch ok (for me being 1.7m only) sufficient, however. Left channel of my speakers was not working, even after exchanging them. Internet not working on my phone, either. No help given to connect, as on earlier flights, instead 'proof' was given to me by a flight attendant that Internet was working on her iphone. Not really helpful. No reading materials. Very basic and little food-options offered in a tiny cardboard-box, 'designed for a reason' (whatever this reason may be). Very inattentive inflight-cabin-crew: As for the after dinner drink I was ignored by the them while my two seat-members got served their drinks of choice. Minimum seat-recline, similar to those in budget-airlines. No soap in washroom. No disinfectant-kits offered as advertised, I got one after request only. Wearing a mask, which is 'mandatory' was not re-enforced, a man in the middle seat was wearing his mask under his chin - no one of the crew cared. Overall the crew seemed to be overworked and de-motivated. The typical glass of champagne offered to me as a PPS-member when I raised my concerns did not really help, it just would have been good enough if the cabin-crew would have fulfilled their duties. Disembarking was fast and swift as well as delivery of my checked-in baggage.
issue: Boarding was slow due to a full flight and crew having to sort passengers and luggage
fixes: Deploy a mobile boarding‑pass verification app (e.g., SITA’s “Passenger Self‑Service”) that lets passengers scan their QR codes at dedicated kiosks, reducing manual check‑in time by up to 40 % per gate. | - Integrate a real‑time seat‑allocation engine into the airline’s reservation system (e.g., Amadeus Altéa) that automatically reassigns seats in response to load factors ≥90 %, cutting passenger‑sorting delays by 15 %. | - Install RFID‑enabled baggage tags and equip ground‑crew with handheld scanners linked to the airport’s Baggage Handling System; this enables instant identification of checked bags and speeds loading/unloading by at least 20 %. | - Launch a pre‑boarding digital queue management system (such as QLess) that sends SMS alerts when it’s your turn, and display live wait times on the gate screen, ensuring a predictable flow even during peak loads.

issue: Loyalty status (Solitaire-PPS) was not recognized on board
fixes: Deploy a real‑time API integration between the loyalty platform (e.g., LoyaltyLion) and the airline’s reservation system (e.g., Sabre Red Workspace) to automatically pull Solitaire‑PPS status into passenger profiles at booking and rebook. | - Configure the airline’s self‑service kiosks with a “Check Your Status” button linked to the loyalty backend via the existing Web Services interface, allowing passengers to verify Solitaire‑PPS before boarding. | - Schedule a weekly “Status Sync” job in Azure Data Factory that pulls the latest loyalty tiers from the PPS database into the CRM (e.g., Salesforce Service Cloud) and updates all relevant records with the latest tier ID. | - Launch a targeted email/SMS trigger (via Twilio SendGrid) 24 hours before departure that includes the passenger’s current Solitaire‑PPS level and any applicable perks, ensuring recognition is communicated proactively.

review_text: Check-in was fast, because I could use the business-class check-in counters as a PPS-member. Otherwise endless queues. Boarding was slow because the flight was full and I boarded late with the crowds. No recognition as a Solitaire-PPS-member on board as before. Cabin crew busy with 'sorting' the passengers and their cabin-luggage to not miss our slot. Seat in a 3-3-3 configuration very narrow, seat-pitch ok (for me being 1.7m only) sufficient, however. Left channel of my speakers was not working, even after exchanging them. Internet not working on my phone, either. No help given to connect, as on earlier flights, instead 'proof' was given to me by a flight attendant that Internet was working on her iphone. Not really helpful. No reading materials. Very basic and little food-options offered in a tiny cardboard-box, 'designed for a reason' (whatever this reason may be). Very inattentive inflight-cabin-crew: As for the after dinner drink I was ignored by the them while my two seat-members got served their drinks of choice. Minimum seat-recline, similar to those in budget-airlines. No soap in washroom. No disinfectant-kits offered as advertised, I got one after request only. Wearing a mask, which is 'mandatory' was not re-enforced, a man in the middle seat was wearing his mask under his chin - no one of the crew cared. Overall the crew seemed to be overworked and de-motivated. The typical glass of champagne offered to me as a PPS-member when I raised my concerns did not really help, it just would have been good enough if the cabin-crew would have fulfilled their duties. Disembarking was fast and swift as well as delivery of my checked-in baggage.
issue: Seat width was narrow in a 3‑3‑3 layout and recline was minimal, similar to budget airlines
fixes: Deploy a seat‑width audit using the 3‑D CAD software SolidWorks to measure actual seat dimensions on existing aircraft; set a target minimum width ≥ 19 inches (48 cm) and generate a corrective‑action plan with a deadline within six months. | - Install adjustable‑angle backrest modules sourced from Recaro that allow passengers to select up to a 20‑degree recline beyond the current 6‑degree default, and program the seat‑control logic through the airline’s existing IFMS (Integrated Flight Management System). | - Launch a pilot program on one route by retrofitting two rows per cabin with wider seats (≥ 21 inches) and track passenger satisfaction scores via the Medallia post‑flight survey, aiming for a ≥ 15 % increase in “seat comfort” ratings after three months. | - Integrate a real‑time seat‑comfort feedback loop into the crew app (e.g., using CrewConnect) where cabin staff record any complaints about narrowness or recline limitations; automatically trigger a service ticket in the airline’s ServiceNow queue for immediate review and replacement of affected seats.

issue: Left audio channel of the seat speakers was non‑functional even after replacement
fixes: Deploy a calibrated headphone‑checking station (e.g., Sennheiser HD 660S) at every gate, with a QR‑code checklist in ServiceNow that requires crew to verify left/right audio before boarding; record pass/fail timestamps for auditability. | - Install a real‑time speaker diagnostic module on the aircraft’s AV system (e.g., Panasonic eX3) that logs impedance readings per seat every minute and triggers a preventive‑maintenance alert when any channel deviates beyond ±15 % from baseline values. | - Implement a “Seat‑Speaker Swap Kit” stocked with identical spare units and a standardized 10‑minute replacement SOP documented in the CrewConnect app, ensuring technicians follow a step‑by‑step visual guide and capture completion photos for quality review. | - Set up a weekly Power BI dashboard that pulls maintenance ticket data, fault codes, and passenger feedback ratings to flag any pattern of left‑channel failures >0.5 % across flights, prompting immediate corrective actions before recurrence.

fixes: Deploy a Wi‑Fi network diagnostic app (e.g., “Airplane Mode” in iOS or Android) at every seat to automatically detect and log connectivity issues within 15 seconds; feed the logs into ServiceNow ITSM for instant alerting to cabin crew. | - Install a real‑time cabin‑connectivity dashboard (Power BI + Azure IoT Hub) that displays signal strength per aircraft section and triggers SMS alerts to ground ops when any seat reports below‑threshold performance. | - Equip cabin crew with portable hotspot devices (e.g., Verizon Jetpacks) configured via AirWatch MDM to activate on demand; set up a QR code‑linked activation workflow in Salesforce Service Cloud so passengers receive step‑by‑step instructions without waiting for crew assistance. | - Add a mandatory “Internet Connectivity Check” checklist item in the flight operations SOP, requiring crew to scan the QR code before departure and record results in the electronic flight bag (EFB); non‑compliance logs are routed to a weekly KPI report in Power BI.
